stem: Let \( p = [[p]] \), and let \( f(x) \) be an irreducible polynomial of degree \( [[d]] \) over the field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \). The quotient ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]}[x]/(f(x)) \) is a field. How many elements does this field have?
solution: Elements of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]}[x]/(f(x)) \) are represented by remainders of degree less than \( [[d]] \): polynomials \( c_{0} + c_{1}x + \dots + c_{[[d]]-1}x^{[[d]]-1} \) with each \( c_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \). There are \( [[p]] \) choices for each of the \( [[d]] \) coefficients, giving \( [[p]]^{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \) elements.
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( p \cdot d \) instead of counting \( p^{d} \) coefficient vectors.
This swaps base and exponent, using \( d^{p} \) instead of \( p^{d} \).
This is \( p^{d} - 1 \), the number of nonzero (invertible) elements, not the size of the whole field.
This uses \( d-1 \) coefficients; remainders modulo a degree-\( d \) polynomial have \( d \) coefficients.
This is one more than the correct number of elements.
This is two more than the correct number of elements.

stem: To build the field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]}[x]/(f(x)) \) with \( [[p]]^{[[n]]} \) elements you need a monic irreducible polynomial \( f(x) \) of degree \( [[n]] \) over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), where \( [[n]] \) is prime. How many such monic irreducible degree-\( [[n]] \) polynomials are there over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \)?
solution: Every element of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]^{[[n]]}} \setminus \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) is a root of exactly one monic irreducible polynomial of degree \( [[n]] \) over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) (since \( [[n]] \) is prime, no smaller factor field sits in between), and each such polynomial has exactly \( [[n]] \) roots (its \( [[n]] \) distinct conjugates). So the count is \( \dfrac{[[p]]^{[[n]]}-[[p]]}{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the division by \( [[n]] \); it counts the elements outside \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) but not the polynomials, since each polynomial accounts for \( [[n]] \) of them.
This divides the WHOLE field size \( p^{n} \) by \( n \) instead of the size minus the \( p \) elements already in the base field.
This uses \( p^{n}-1 \) (excluding only \( 0 \)) instead of \( p^{n}-p \) (excluding the whole base field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \)) in the numerator.
This omits the division by \( [[n]] \) and also fails to remove the base-field elements.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one fewer than the correct count.

stem: Let \( f(x) \) be an irreducible polynomial of degree \( [[d]] \) over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), and let \( F = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]}[x]/(f(x)) \). The constant polynomials form a copy of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) sitting inside \( F \). How many elements of \( F \) are genuinely new, i.e. lie outside this copy of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \)?
solution: \( F \) has \( [[p]]^{[[d]]} \) elements in total, and exactly \( [[p]] \) of them (the constant remainders \( 0,1,\dots,[[p]]-1 \)) belong to the embedded copy of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \). So \( [[p]]^{[[d]]} - [[p]] = [[answer]] \) elements lie outside it.
distractor_rationales: This removes only the single element \( 0 \) instead of all \( [[p]] \) constant remainders.
This is \( p^{d-1} \), the count of remainders with a fixed leading coefficient, not the count outside the base field.
This is the size of the whole field \( F \), without removing the base-field elements at all.
This subtracts \( p \) from \( d \) instead of from \( p^{d} \).
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one fewer than the correct count.

stem: Let \( f(x) \) be irreducible of degree \( [[d]] \) over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), and let \( F = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]}[x]/(f(x)) \), a field with \( [[p]]^{[[d]]} \) elements. Since \( [[k]] \) divides \( [[d]] \), \( F \) contains a unique subfield isomorphic to the field with \( [[p]]^{[[k]]} \) elements. How many elements does this subfield have?
solution: A finite field with \( p^{d} \) elements contains, for each divisor \( k \) of \( d \), a unique subfield with \( p^{k} \) elements. Here \( k = [[k]] \) divides \( d = [[d]] \), so the subfield has \( [[p]]^{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \) elements.
distractor_rationales: This is the size of the WHOLE field \( F \), not the smaller subfield.
This uses the wrong exponent \( d/k \) instead of \( k \) itself.
This gives the exponent ratio \( d/k \) rather than \( p^{k} \), the number of elements.
This multiplies \( p \cdot k \) instead of raising \( p \) to the power \( k \).
This is one more than the correct number of elements.
This is one fewer than the correct number of elements.
